_verification_note: Drafted by structural derivation from BS 7671:2018+A2 (KS 1700:2018 adopts BS 7671 substantially per Annex E). Clause numbers and structural content match BS template; engineering values reproduced verbatim. Final verification against the published KS 1700:2018 PDF required.
_ks_adoption_pathway: KS 1700:2018 Annex E §V — adopts BS 7671:2018+A2 Reg 521 verbatim

_ks_deviation_from_bs.summary: KS Annex G defines Kenya climate zones with ambient temperature baselines higher than the BS 7671 30°C indoor default

highland.description: Above 1500m altitude — Nairobi, Nakuru, Eldoret, Nyeri

coastal.description: Sea-level, high humidity — Mombasa, Kilifi, Lamu

arid_northern.description: Below 800m altitude, dry climate — Marsabit, Garissa, Wajir

default._note: Conservative default in absence of zone declaration

_ks_deviation_from_bs.engineering_impact: Cable ampacity derating coefficient Ca applied more often than in UK installations; effective cable size larger for same load. Apply highland zone defaults if site is at altitude ≥1500m; coastal zone if within 10km of Indian Ocean / Lake Victoria shore

_note: The installation method determines the current-carrying capacity table to use from Appendix 4 (see appendix4-cable-ratings.json). Always select the method that matches the ACTUAL installation, not the most favourable.

A1.name: Single cables in conduit in a thermally insulating wall
A1.description: Insulated single-core conductors run inside a conduit which itself is embedded within a thermally insulating wall (e.g. timber stud wall with mineral wool insulation). Heat dissipation is poor.
A1.typical_application: Domestic surface wiring inside insulated stud partition walls.
A1.rating_impact: Lowest ratings — poor heat dissipation. Use Table 4D2A column A1.
A1.design_notes: Common in modern timber-frame buildings. Often missed by designers using Method C ratings — leads to under-sized cables.

A2.name: Multi-core cable in conduit in a thermally insulating wall
A2.description: Multi-core sheathed cable inside conduit, conduit within insulated wall.
A2.typical_application: Domestic refurbishment where cables are pulled through new insulated partition.
A2.rating_impact: Slightly higher than A1 due to better thermal coupling within the cable bundle.

B1.name: Single cables in conduit on a wall or in trunking
B1.description: Insulated single-core conductors in conduit mounted on the wall surface, or in trunking. Conduit/trunking is exposed to room air.
B1.typical_application: Commercial offices with surface trunking, schools, industrial control wiring.
B1.rating_impact: Better than A1/A2 — heat can dissipate from conduit surface. Use Table 4D2A column B1.

B2.name: Multi-core cable in conduit on a wall or in trunking
B2.description: Multi-core sheathed cable inside conduit or trunking on wall.
B2.typical_application: Most common method for office final circuits. T&E or LSZH multi-core in dado trunking, conduit, or in trunking above ceiling.
B2.rating_impact: Reference method for most commercial work. Use Table 4D2B column B2.
B2.design_notes: Beware of grouping — multiple circuits in same trunking attract grouping factor Cg.

C.name: Clipped direct to a non-metallic surface
C.description: Cable clipped directly to a wall, ceiling, or other surface without any enclosing conduit or trunking. Cable is exposed to free air.
C.typical_application: Plant rooms, basement runs, industrial premises, exposed installations.
C.rating_impact: Good — cable dissipates heat freely. Use Table 4D2B column C.
C.design_notes: Most generous ratings short of free air. Often the default in pricing assumptions — verify actual install matches.

D1.name: Multi-core cable in a buried conduit (ducted)
D1.description: Cable run inside a conduit buried in the ground. Conduit fills with air or is back-filled with sand. Depth typically 0.7m.
D1.typical_application: Underground submains, site lighting feeds, external power supplies from MSB to outbuilding.
D1.rating_impact: Lower than D2 due to air gap reducing thermal coupling. Use Table 4D2B column D1.
D1.typical_cable: SWA in plastic ducting, MV mains in concrete duct.
D1.design_notes: Ducting allows future cable replacement without excavation — preferred method for permanent underground routes.

D2.name: Multi-core cable direct in ground
D2.description: Cable laid directly in the ground (sand bedding, marker tile above, backfill).
D2.typical_application: Direct buried submains, street lighting cabling, distribution network cables.
D2.rating_impact: Better than D1 — direct soil contact gives better thermal dissipation. Use Table 4D2B/4D4A column D2.
D2.typical_cable: SWA (steel wire armoured) — must be armoured for direct burial.
D2.design_notes: Apply soil thermal resistivity correction (see appendix4-correction-factors.json). Default tables assume 2.5 K·m/W.

E.name: Multi-core cable in free air or on a perforated horizontal cable tray
E.description: Cable suspended in free air, or laid on a perforated tray with free air below and beside it. Cables spaced sufficiently to not affect each other.
E.typical_application: Plant rooms, risers with cable tray, industrial overhead routes, computer rooms with cable trays.
E.rating_impact: Highest ratings for multi-core cable. Use Table 4D2B column E.
E.design_notes: Apply grouping factor when cables are touching on tray. Apply 'in air' grouping factors, not buried.

F.name: Single cables in free air or on a perforated horizontal cable tray (touching)
F.description: Three single-core cables laid touching in trefoil or flat formation, on cable tray with free air below.
F.typical_application: Submain feeders, generator outputs, LV mains where single-core gives more flexibility.
F.rating_impact: Lower than spaced single-core but highest for single-core touching. Use Table 4D2A column F.
F.design_notes: For high-current submains (>250A), single-core touching is often the practical choice. Beware of skin effect and proximity losses above 300mm².

G.name: Single cables in free air, spaced
G.description: Single-core cables spaced apart by at least one cable diameter on a tray or hangers.
G.typical_application: Very large submain feeders, generator interconnections, transformer secondary feeds.
G.rating_impact: Highest ratings of all installation methods. Tabulated separately in Table 4D2A column G.
G.design_notes: Spacing significantly improves rating but requires more tray width — physical constraint often.

wiring_system_selection_factors.ambient_conditions: Temperature, humidity, presence of water (IP rating).
wiring_system_selection_factors.fire_resistance: Required IEC 60331 rating for life safety circuits — see cable-types-fire-rated.json.
wiring_system_selection_factors.chemical_exposure: Resistant sheaths needed for corrosive environments (LSZH for low smoke, special compounds for hydrocarbon exposure).
wiring_system_selection_factors.flammability: LSZH (Low Smoke Zero Halogen) preferred for buildings with assembly use — reduces toxic fume on fire.
wiring_system_selection_factors.future_flexibility: Trunking and conduit allow re-cabling; direct-buried does not. Match to building lifecycle.
wiring_system_selection_factors.EMI_immunity: Separate routes for power and data (see Reg 528.1). Steel armour or screened cable for sensitive runs.

cable_segregation.principle: Circuits of different categories shall be segregated to prevent mutual interference.

categories.category_2: ELV signal circuits — fire alarm, security, intruder alarm, BMS, data networks
categories.category_3: Other ELV — call systems, telecoms (low-power), audio

segregation_rules: Category 1 and Category 2: separate cables, separate compartments of trunking OR ≥150mm physical separation, unless cables are screened
Fire alarm and emergency lighting: dedicated containment, fire-rated for the duration required (typically FP200 or FP400)
Data networks: parallel runs with mains must be separated per TIA-569 / EN 50174 — typically 200mm for unscreened, less for screened

fixings_and_supports.thermal_movement: Cable supports must accommodate thermal expansion. Continuous support (clipped direct, in conduit) preferred for long runs.

fire_collapse_prevention.rule: Cable supports throughout the installation must be such that cables are not liable to premature collapse in the event of a fire.
fire_collapse_prevention.implication: Plastic cable clips alone are non-compliant on escape routes — metal fixings required.
fire_collapse_prevention.common_solutions: P-clips with metal saddle, fire-rated cable clips (BS 8519 compliant), metal cable basket with metallic supports.

specific_environments.bathrooms: Cables outside zones can use any method. Within zones, only mechanically protected (in conduit or under floor) acceptable.
specific_environments.underfloor_heating: Maintain separation from heating cables. Use higher temperature rating cables if running through warm zones.
specific_environments.agricultural: Mechanical protection mandatory — animals, vehicles. Conduit or SWA standard.
specific_environments.construction_sites: Mechanical protection mandatory. 30mA RCD on all socket outlets. Cable hangers or burial preferred.
specific_environments.swimming_pools: Special restrictions in Zones 0, 1, 2. Specialised cable types and methods (see Section 702).
